data.title: Billerud AB Non-GAAP EPS of -SEK 0.69, revenue of SEK 9.82B; gives Q2 outlook
data.description:
• Billerud AB (publ) press release (BLRDY): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of -SEK 0.69.
  • Revenue of SEK 9.82B (-11.5% Y/Y).
  • Sequential sales volume growth of 9%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA SEK 525 million; Adjusted EBITDA margin 5%.
  • The board of directors proposes a dividend of SEK 2.00 (3.50) per share for the year 2025.
  • Outlook for Q2: Benefits from lower pulpwood prices and cost-saving program but challenging market conditions in Europe. Solid market conditions in North America. Broad-based price increases in both regions to mitigate cost inflation for chemicals and logistics. Sequentially higher annual maintenance shutdown costs.

data.title: BP Non-GAAP EPADS of $1.24 beats by $0.31, revenue of $52.26B beats by $6.3B; gives Q2 and reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• BP p.l.c. press release (BP): Q1 Non-GAAP EPADS of $1.24 beats by $0.31.
  • Revenue of $52.26B (+11.4% Y/Y) beats by $6.3B.
  • Operating cash flow for the first quarter was $2.9 billion, compared with $2.8 billion for the same period in 2025.
  • Capital expenditure in the first quarter was $3.3 billion, compared with $3.6 billion in the same period of 2025.
  • At the end of the first quarter, net debt was $25.3 billion, compared with $22.2 billion at the end of the fourth quarter 2025 and $27.0 billion at the end of the first quarter 2025.
  • "We reiterate our 2026 capital expenditure budget in the range of $13-13.5 billion."
  • 2Q 2026 guidance: Looking ahead, bp expects second quarter 2026 reported upstream production to be lower compared with the first quarter 2026, due to seasonal maintenance predominantly in the Gulf of America and the effects of disruption in the Middle East. The heightened volatility in the oil and gas prices could also impact PSA contracts.

data.title: Elon Musk vs. Sam Altman: AI clash heads to court
data.description:
Technology tycoons Elon Musk and Sam Altman are poised to face off in a high-stakes trial revolving around the alleged betrayal, deceit and unbridled ambition that blurred the bickering billionaires’ once-shared vision for the development of artificial intelligence.

The trial, which started Monday with jury selection, centers on the 2015 birth of ChatGPT maker OpenAI (OPENAI) as a nonprofit startup primarily funded by Musk before evolving into a capitalistic venture now valued at $852 billion.

The trial’s outcome could sway the balance of power in AI - breakthrough technology that is increasingly being feared as a potential job killer and an existential threat to humanity’s survival.

Those perceived risks are among the reasons that Musk, the world’s richest person, cites for filing an August 2024 lawsuit that will now be decided by a jury and U.S. District Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers in Oakland, California.

The civil lawsuit accuses Altman, OpenAI’s CEO, and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, of double-crossing Musk by straying from the San Francisco company’s founding mission to be an altruistic steward of a revolutionary technology. The lawsuit alleges they shifted into a moneymaking mode behind his back.

The AP contributed to this report.

data.title: Mission Bancorp declares MISSING dividend
data.description:
• Mission Bancorp (OTCPK:MSBC) declares declares a 5% annual stock dividend..

data.title: Dynatrace rises after Starboard takes stake, pushes for changes
data.description:
• Dynatrace (DT) stock price jumped about 8% on Tuesday pre-market trade after a report that activist hedge fund Starboard Value has taken a significant stake and is pushing for strategic changes.
  • The activist investor is pushing for changes that could help boost the stock, according to a Wall Street Journal report that cites a draft letter. The letter, authored by managing member Peter Feld, is expected to be delivered on Tuesday.
  • Starboard, now one of Dynatrace’s (DT) top five shareholders, is urging the company to accelerate its share buyback program and push margins higher, arguing the stock trades at a discount to software peers.
  • The move comes after Dynatrace’s (DT) shares had fallen more than 17% year‑to‑date, amid concerns over slowing revenue growth and short‑term execution.

data.title: Air Liquide posts €6.79B in Q1 revenue; targets +100 bps margin expansion and net profit growth in 2026
LendingClub projects $3.0B-$3.1B Q2 2026 originations while launching home improvement loans and keeping $1.65-$1.80 EPS outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LendingClub (LC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We had a great start to 2026, delivering 31% year-on-year growth in originations to $2.7 billion, while achieving record pretax earnings of $67 million and a return on tangible common equity of 14.5%." (CEO & Director Scott Sanborn)
  • "We're also delivering our key strategic priorities, including expanding into the new home improvement vertical, driving AI-enabled operating efficiency and introducing the upcoming rebrand to Happen Bank." (CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • "As of this month, we started underwriting and issuing home improvement loans through our inaugural partnership with Wisetack, an embedded platform that reaches over 40,000 contractors." (CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • "Over 90% of loan issuance is now fully automated, requiring no human intervention." (CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • "Loan originations grew by 31% to $2.7 billion, above the high end of our guidance range." (Chief Financial Officer Andrew LaBenne)
  • "Net interest income increased 18% to $176 million, another all-time high." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Outlook

  • "We expect to deliver loan originations of $3.0 billion to $3.1 billion" in Q2 2026 and "diluted earnings per share of $0.40 to $0.45." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "For the full year, we continue to expect originations of $11.6 billion to $12.6 billion and diluted EPS of $1.65 to $1.80" and management said this was based on "the assumption of a stable consumer and rate environment." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "As a reminder, we were assuming 75 basis points in cuts when we entered the year... which we no longer expect to benefit from." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management maintained the same full-year originations and EPS ranges, while updating rate assumptions to "0" Fed cuts for the remainder of the year. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Financial results

  • "Noninterest income was $76 million, up 12% year-over-year and down sequentially" and management attributed the sequential decline to the move to fair value option and higher benchmark rates late in the quarter. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "Risk-adjusted revenue... grew 58% to $252 million" which management tied to revenue growth and "materially lower provision for credit losses under fair value options." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "The net interest margin expanded to 6.3%, up 30 basis points over the prior quarter" and management said it expected NIM "to return to around 6% as we progress through 2026" given the market-implied path for Fed cuts. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "Provision for credit losses was less than $1 million" and "our net charge-off ratio... improved meaningfully to 3.5%, down from 6.1%." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "Overall, pretax net income was $67 million" and "diluted earnings per share was $0.44" while "expenses... were $185 million" with marketing affected by fair value accounting and growth investments. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • "Since inception and through the first quarter, we have utilized $38 million" of the $100 million repurchase/acquisition program and "reduced our average diluted share count by 1.5 million shares compared to the previous quarter." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Q&A

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked how quickly additional home improvement partnerships could be realized vs. Wisetack; CEO & Director Sanborn said inbound interest reflected demand for "a stable bank balance sheet" plus "the flexibility of a fintech," and added "implementing additional partnerships... will be roughly less than half the work" after the first integration, with "the bigger contribution... next year."
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked how to think about marketing expense and rebrand costs; CEO & Director Sanborn said rebrand costs were "primarily sort of operational in nature" (touchpoints like "e-mails and call center scripts and web pages and mobile app"), and CFO LaBenne said marketing "should ramp roughly with volume" and increase alongside seasonal volume in Q2 and Q3.
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about credit impacts from the "Iran war" and rising oil; CEO & Director Sanborn said, "No. We haven't seen anything in leading indicators" while noting the company would be "carefully watching" inflation spillovers.
  • William Ryan, Seaport: asked about loan sale pricing trends and buyer mix; CFO LaBenne said buyers were "very steady" but noted Q1 deals were "priced before the Iran war" and if benchmarks stay higher "we would expect prices to come down solely because of the changes in benchmarks," which management said was embedded in guidance.
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: asked why guidance implied flatter quarterly earnings power; CFO LaBenne pointed to the shift from an assumption of "3 Fed cuts" to "0" (a "headwind to revenue") alongside continued investments, while reiterating confidence in the full-year EPS range.
  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP: asked about agentic AI search risks/opportunities; CEO & Director Sanborn said LendingClub’s obstacle was "inertia" and added, "if the agents evolve... we think we're a net beneficiary," while planning to increase content to show up in AI-driven discovery.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on rate-driven headwinds, fair value marks, and whether earnings would stay flat, including "it kind of implies that earnings power remains relatively flat" (Vincent Caintic, BTIG).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on macro/rates, using conditional language such as "if no change to rates from here" and "we'll be carefully watching" while still emphasizing execution and demand. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne; CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • Versus Q4 2025, management language shifted from launching home improvement "midyear" to being live and issuing, while tone on rates turned more defensive about the loss of expected cuts (Q4: "we were assuming 75 basis points in cuts"; Q1: "we now assume... 0"). (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The home improvement initiative moved from "on track to launch... midyear" (Q4 2025) to "started underwriting and issuing" with Wisetack (Q1 2026), and management framed 2026 as "laying the pipes" with larger contribution "next year." (CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • Guidance ranges for full-year 2026 originations ($11.6 billion to $12.6 billion) and diluted EPS ($1.65 to $1.80) were reiterated, but rate expectations shifted from cuts being a potential tailwind (Q4) to "no additional Fed rate cuts this year" being a modeled headwind (Q1). (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • Profitability and credit metrics discussed in Q1 reflected the fair value transition’s impacts (e.g., provision and fair value adjustments), whereas Q4 focused on previewing those mechanics and providing modeling estimates. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Risks and concerns

  • "Higher benchmark rates... increased fair value adjustments" and management cautioned that higher benchmarks can pressure loan sale prices even if credit performance remains solid. (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)
  • Management highlighted geopolitical and inflation risk monitoring, saying the company was watching "how might that affect the broader inflationary cycle and impact on consumers." (CEO & Director Sanborn)
  • Execution risk on new verticals and spend was acknowledged as investment-heavy, with management citing ongoing investments in "new marketing channels, supporting the rebrand and building out home improvement." (Chief Financial Officer LaBenne)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a record profitability quarter alongside 31% origination growth to $2.7 billion, with the rebrand to Happen Bank and the launch of home improvement lending through Wisetack as the key strategic developments. Guidance was reiterated for full-year originations of $11.6 billion to $12.6 billion and diluted EPS of $1.65 to $1.80, while management updated its rate framework to assume no Fed cuts and positioned higher benchmarks as a near-term headwind to pricing and fair value marks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
